ontology.foodex2_description: Sugar and sugar products — raw sugar (cane)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 74801000 maps to 'Sugar (substance)' as the closest defensible parent match; no specific raw-sugar SNOMED CT code was identified in the SNOMED CT hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw sugar is approximately 1 teaspoon (4 g), commonly added to beverages such as tea or coffee.

nutrient_source.title: Raw sugar — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw sugar (F13030008). All per-100g nutrient values are sourced from HPB with confidence 0.95. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Sugars, raw' (FDC ID 169655) was cross-referenced and is consistent with HPB values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 65 for raw/turbinado sugar is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 3.99) / 100 ≈ 2.6 for a 4 g teaspoon serving (3.99 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high added-sugar content (99.8 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre; AHA 2018 guidelines identify high added-sugar intake as an independent driver of blood pressure elevation via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should eliminate or strictly minimise raw sugar intake; despite its very low sodium content (1 mg/100 g), the 99.8 g/100 g sugar load drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and should be replaced with whole-fruit sources of sweetness that provide fibre and potassium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Raw sugar is 99.8 g carbohydrate/100 g, virtually all free sugar, with zero dietary fibre and a GI of 65 (medium), producing a rapid and substantial postprandial glucose spike that far exceeds ADA added-sugar thresholds.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or eliminate raw sugar from their diet; even small quantities (e.g., 1–2 teaspoons in beverages) significantly raise postprandial blood glucose, and low-GI non-nutritive sweeteners such as stevia or erythritol should be used if sweetness is required.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar intake (99.8 g/100 g) with zero fibre, zero beneficial fats, and no cardioprotective micronutrients; excess dietary sugar is independently associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular mortality per AHA 2016 scientific statement.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should strictly limit raw sugar consumption, as its high free-sugar content promotes hypertriglyceridaemia, hepatic de novo lipogenesis, and endothelial dysfunction; the AHA recommends no more than 6 teaspoons (25 g) of added sugar per day for women and 9 teaspoons (38 g) for men.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Raw sugar at 99.8 g sugar/100 g with zero fibre and no anti-inflammatory components promotes pro-inflammatory mediators including TNF-alpha and IL-6, worsening RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should eliminate added sugar from their diet and replace it with anti-inflammatory flavour enhancers such as cinnamon or ginger, as high sugar intake is directly associated with increased RA disease activity and systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw sugar is a minimally refined sweetener produced from sugarcane (Saccharum officinarum) juice that has been clarified, evaporated, and centrifuged to remove most but not all of the molasses, yielding light golden-brown crystals with a subtle molasses aroma and a clean, intensely sweet flavour. Unlike white refined sugar, raw sugar retains trace amounts of minerals including potassium (21 mg/100 g), calcium (7 mg/100 g), and iron (0.3 mg/100 g), though these quantities are nutritionally insignificant at typical serving sizes. Nutritionally, raw sugar is almost entirely free sugar: 99.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which 99.8 g is sugar, with zero dietary fibre, zero protein, and zero fat. Its energy density is 382 kcal per 100 g. The glycemic index is 65 (medium category per University of Sydney GI Database), but the glycemic load per standard 1-teaspoon (4 g) serving is approximately 2.6 — the concern arises from cumulative daily intake rather than a single small serving. Raw sugar is used globally as a table sweetener, in baking, confectionery, beverages, and cooking. It is marketed as a more 'natural' alternative to white sugar, but for clinical purposes it is nutritionally equivalent to refined white sugar. It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its extremely high free-sugar content and absence of any protective nutrients. Common trade names include turbinado sugar and demerara sugar.
common_user_queries: Is raw sugar healthier than white sugar?
How many calories in raw sugar?
What is the GI of raw sugar?
Is raw sugar suitable for hypertension?
Is demerara sugar the same as raw sugar?
How much sugar is in raw sugar per 100g?
meal_context: Table sweetener for tea and coffee

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F13030008) with confidence 0.95, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw sugar (FDC ID 169655). GI value of 65 is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. All values are internally consistent and well-supported by two independent approved sources.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero — raw sugar is a pure carbohydrate product containing no protein by composition. fat_g is naturally zero — raw sugar contains no fat by composition; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (F13030008) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all confirmed zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — all fibre is removed during processing; confirmed per HPB Foodo. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero — plant-derived product contains no cholesterol. Negative sentinel values: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 as detailed vitamin and mineral data beyond those reported by HPB are not available in the HPB Foodo database for this entry; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169655) reports these as not determined or trace for raw sugar. water_g is set to 0.5 based on USDA SR Legacy data for raw sugar (approximately 0.5 g/100 g residual moisture). Ontology note: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A000E are the best defensible matches identified; SNOMED CT 74801000 is used as the closest parent match (maps to 'Sugar (substance)') as no specific raw-sugar SNOMED CT code was identified. GI note: The input record cited 'Embedded GI Database' as source; this has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) as the approved GI source per Project Veritas source policy.

Iteration 3: Confirmed snomed_ct_match_type is 'closest_parent' — one of the three explicitly allowed values. All hard gates now pass. No further changes required.
